questions.question:
The most efficient algorithm for finding the number of connected components in an undirected graph on n vertices and m edges has time complexity
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(m)
C. Theta(m+n)
D. Theta(mn)
questions.to_find: The time complexity of the most efficient algorithm to find connected components in an undirected graph

understand.plain: We are given an undirected graph with n vertices and m edges, and we want to count how many connected components it has. We need to find which algorithm does this most efficiently. BFS or DFS visits every vertex once and traverses every edge once, making it the standard approach.

keywords.explain: A maximal set of vertices in an undirected graph such that there is a path between every pair of vertices in the set.
keywords.example: In a graph with vertices {1,2,3,4} and edges {(1,2),(2,3)}, the components are {1,2,3} and {4}.

keywords.explain: Graph traversal algorithms that visit each vertex and edge systematically; BFS uses a queue, DFS uses a stack or recursion.
keywords.example: Starting DFS from vertex 1 in the above graph visits 1->2->3, identifying one component.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='340' height='160' viewBox='0 0 340 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='130' height='140' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='75' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Component 1</text><circle cx='45' cy='70' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='45' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><circle cx='115' cy='70' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='115' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><circle cx='80' cy='130' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><line x1='63' y1='70' x2='97' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='50' y1='87' x2='72' y2='113' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='180' y='10' width='140' height='140' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Component 2</text><circle cx='215' cy='90' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='215' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><circle cx='295' cy='90' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='295' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><line x1='233' y1='90' x2='277' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='170' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>isolated</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An undirected graph split into two connected components: one with vertices 1-2-3 connected in a triangle, and another with vertices 4-5 connected by an edge.

given.aim: Find the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm to count connected components in an undirected graph with n vertices and m edges.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in the graph
terms.example: n=5 means the graph has 5 nodes
terms.connects: Each vertex must be visited at least once, contributing O(n) to the complexity

terms.meaning: Number of edges in the graph
terms.example: m=4 means 4 edges connect various pairs of vertices
terms.connects: Each edge is examined at most twice in undirected BFS/DFS, contributing O(m)

terms.meaning: Standard graph traversal algorithms used to find all reachable vertices from a given source
terms.example: Run BFS from an unvisited vertex; all visited vertices form one component
terms.connects: Running BFS/DFS repeatedly over all unvisited vertices counts all components

given.plan: Use BFS or DFS starting from each unvisited vertex. Each execution of BFS/DFS marks one complete connected component. The total work across all executions is proportional to the total number of vertices and edges visited, giving Theta(n+m).

steps.title: Identify the algorithm
steps.apply: To find connected components in an undirected graph, we run BFS or DFS. Start from any unvisited vertex, traverse all reachable vertices (one component), then move to the next unvisited vertex and repeat.
steps.note: This works because BFS/DFS from a vertex visits exactly its connected component.

steps.title: Analyze the time complexity
steps.apply: BFS/DFS visits each vertex exactly once: O(n) total. Each edge is examined at most twice (once from each endpoint) in an undirected graph: O(m) total. So total time is Theta(n + m). No algorithm can do better since it must read the input, which itself is Omega(n + m).
steps.note: Theta(m+n) is tight: both vertices and edges determine the work. If m >> n, it is Theta(m); if n >> m (sparse), it is Theta(n). In general it is Theta(n+m).

solution.result: The most efficient algorithm for finding connected components runs in Theta(m+n) time. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: BFS/DFS traversal of a graph with n vertices and m edges runs in Theta(n+m) time, which is optimal since the input size itself is Theta(n+m).

questions.question: Let G be a graph with 100! vertices, with each vertex labelled by a distinct permutation of the numbers 1, 2, ..., 100. There is an edge between vertices u and v if and only if the label of v can be obtained by swapping two adjacent numbers in the label of u. Let d denote the degree of a vertex in G, and z denote the number of connected components of G. Then, g = 10 * z =
questions.to_find: The value of g = 10 * z, where z is the number of connected components of the permutation graph G

understand.plain: We build a graph where each node is a permutation of {1,...,100}. Two nodes are adjacent if one permutation can be obtained from the other by swapping two neighboring elements (like swapping positions i and i+1). We need to find how many connected components this graph has. Since any permutation can be reached from any other by a sequence of adjacent swaps (bubble sort uses only adjacent swaps), the whole graph is connected, giving z=1. So g = 10*1 = 10.

keywords.explain: A swap of two elements that are next to each other in a sequence, i.e., swapping positions i and i+1.
A maximal subset of vertices where every pair of vertices is reachable from each other via edges.
keywords.example: If all 100! permutations can reach each other via adjacent swaps, the entire graph is one component.

keywords.explain: Bubble sort sorts any permutation using only adjacent swaps. The permutation graph here is the Cayley graph of S_n with adjacent transpositions as generators.
keywords.example: To sort (3,1,2): swap positions 1-2 -> (1,3,2), then swap 2-3 -> (1,2,3). All permutations are connected.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='160' viewBox='0 0 360 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='55' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(1,2,3)</text><rect x='130' y='10' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='175' y='31' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(2,1,3)</text><rect x='130' y='100' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='175' y='121' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(1,3,2)</text><rect x='250' y='55' width='90' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='295' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>(3,2,1)</text><line x1='100' y1='75' x2='130' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='85' x2='130' y2='115' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='35' x2='250' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='125' x2='250' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>All 3!=6 permutations: one connected component</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Small example with n=3: the 6 permutations of {1,2,3} form one connected component via adjacent swaps, analogous to the n=100 case.

given.aim: Find the number z of connected components of the 100!-vertex permutation graph (edges = adjacent transpositions) and compute g = 10*z.

terms.meaning: Each vertex is one of the 100! distinct permutations of {1,2,...,100}
terms.connects: The graph has 100! nodes, but connectivity depends on reachability via adjacent swaps

terms.meaning: An edge exists between two permutations if one can be obtained from the other by swapping elements at positions i and i+1 for some i in {1,...,99}
terms.connects: This defines the edge relation determining the graph's structure

terms.meaning: Any permutation can be sorted to (1,2,...,100) using only adjacent swaps (as in bubble sort), so any two permutations are connected
terms.example: To reach (3,1,2) from (1,2,3): reverse path of bubble sort steps connects them
terms.connects: This proves the graph is connected, so z=1

given.plan: Show that all 100! permutations lie in one connected component using the fact that any permutation can be sorted (or built) using adjacent transpositions, then compute g = 10 * z = 10 * 1 = 10.

steps.title: Show the graph is connected (z = 1)
steps.formula_raw: Any permutation sigma reachable from identity via adjacent swaps
steps.apply: Given any permutation p = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_100), we can perform bubble sort using only adjacent swaps to transform p into the identity permutation (1,2,...,100). Bubble sort uses only adjacent swaps. Therefore there is a path (sequence of edges) from any permutation p to the identity. Since any two permutations p and q both connect to the identity, they are connected to each other. Hence the entire graph G is one single connected component: z = 1.
steps.note: The permutation graph generated by adjacent transpositions is the Cayley graph of the symmetric group S_n; it is always connected for n >= 1.

steps.title: Compute g = 10 * z
steps.apply: Since z = 1 (only one connected component), g = 10 * 1 = 10.
steps.note: The question asks for g = 10z, and with z=1, the answer is 10.

solution.result: z = 1 (the graph is connected), so g = 10 * z = 10.

questions.formula_note: Cayley graph of the symmetric group S_n with adjacent transpositions as generators is always connected; any permutation is reachable from the identity via adjacent swaps (bubble sort principle).

questions.to_find: The correct decomposition of the given directed graph into strongly connected components (SCCs)

understand.plain: A strongly connected component (SCC) is a maximal set of vertices where you can travel from any vertex to any other vertex following directed edges. We need to identify which vertices can mutually reach each other. Vertices in a cycle form an SCC. Vertices with only one-way reachability belong to different SCCs.

keywords.explain: A maximal subset of vertices in a directed graph such that every vertex in the subset is reachable from every other vertex in the subset via directed paths.
keywords.example: If A->B->C->A forms a cycle, then {A,B,C} is an SCC since each can reach the others.

keywords.explain: A path in a directed graph that starts and ends at the same vertex, following edge directions.
keywords.example: P->Q->R->P is a directed cycle, so P, Q, R are in the same SCC.

understand.visual_alt: Directed graph: P, Q, R form a cycle (P->Q->R->P). Q->S, S->T->U->S form another cycle. R->V points to isolated V. SCCs: {P,Q,R}, {S,T,U}, {V}.

given.aim: Identify the strongly connected components of the given directed graph.

terms.meaning: Directed edges P->Q, Q->R, R->P form a directed 3-cycle
terms.example: From P you can reach Q, then R, then back to P
terms.connects: All three vertices P, Q, R can reach each other, forming SCC {P,Q,R}

terms.meaning: Directed edges S->T, T->U, U->S form a directed 3-cycle
terms.example: From S you reach T, then U, then back to S
terms.connects: Vertices S, T, U can all reach each other, forming SCC {S,T,U}

terms.meaning: V has only incoming edges (from R) and no outgoing edges back to any other vertex
terms.example: R->V exists, but no edge V->anything, so V cannot reach R
terms.connects: V cannot be in the same SCC as any other vertex; it forms singleton SCC {V}

given.plan: Identify directed cycles: P-Q-R-P is a cycle => SCC {P,Q,R}. S-T-U-S is a cycle => SCC {S,T,U}. V has no outgoing edges to complete a cycle => SCC {V}. Verify: Q->S is a cross-SCC edge (not within an SCC).

steps.title: Identify SCC containing P
steps.formula_raw: u and v in same SCC iff u reaches v AND v reaches u
steps.apply: From P: P->Q->R->P (cycle). P reaches Q and R; Q reaches R and P; R reaches P and Q. So {P, Q, R} is an SCC. Also Q->S, R->V are edges going out of this SCC but S and V cannot reach back to P, Q, or R.
steps.note: A directed cycle means all vertices in the cycle are in the same SCC.

steps.title: Identify SCC containing S
steps.apply: From S: S->T->U->S (cycle). S reaches T and U; T reaches U and S; U reaches S and T. So {S, T, U} is an SCC. None of S, T, U have edges going back to {P,Q,R}.
steps.note: Q->S is a bridge edge from SCC {P,Q,R} to SCC {S,T,U} in the condensation DAG.

steps.title: Identify SCC for V
steps.apply: V is reached from R (R->V). But V has no outgoing edges. Therefore V cannot reach any other vertex. So V is a singleton SCC {V}.
steps.note: Vertices with in-degree > 0 but no outgoing edges back toward their predecessors always form singleton SCCs (sinks in the condensation DAG).

solution.result: The SCCs are {P,Q,R}, {S,T,U}, and {V}. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Kosaraju's or Tarjan's SCC algorithm: vertices form an SCC if and only if they lie on a common directed cycle. Vertices without a return path to their predecessors form singleton SCCs.
